talk_metadata.talk_title: Mechanisms of resistance to direct RAS inhibitors and approaches for overcoming resistance
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we have many inhibitors of the oncogenes, over 100 kinase inhibitors, new KRAS inhibitors that I'll mention a little bit of"
Speaker said: "we see a single example of a complete response in this waterfall plot, but then you notice a patient responded, but then had progressive disease and relapsed"
Speaker said: "the promise of it, but getting that to play out correctly, of course, is a big challenge"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Emergence of resistant subclones (e.g., KRAS G12D) that share neoantigens but are not targeted by G12C‑selective drugs
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Speaker said: "you see a patient responded, but then had progressive disease and relapsed"
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Speaker noted a single complete response among many patients, indicating a subset of exceptional responders

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that allele‑specific KRAS G12C inhibitors spare T cells (observation) but concluded that “selective killing of even a subpopulation will alert the immune system” without providing data that T‑cell function is truly preserved (contradiction)

key_data_gaps: No biomarker‑stratified analysis beyond KRAS allele
Limited long‑term follow‑up on durability of responses

crispro_opportunity.description: Target the resistant KRAS G12D subclone and shared neoantigens using CrisPRO‑guided neoantigen vaccine or T‑cell receptor engineering
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described that resistant G12D cells share common neoantigens with G12C‑sensitive cells and that combination with checkpoint blockade improved complete responses
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict and prioritize shared neoantigen peptides for personalized vaccine design to eliminate resistant subclones

crispro_opportunity.description: Implement early ctDNA monitoring to detect low‑frequency resistant KRAS alleles (<0.01%) before clinical progression
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that even 0.01% resistant cells can drive relapse in mouse models
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate ultra‑deep sequencing data to flag emerging resistant clones for early therapeutic switch

cited_competitors.name: sotorasib
cited_competitors.context: Approved KRAS G12C inhibitor referenced as a benchmark

cited_competitors.name: adagrasib
cited_competitors.context: Approved KRAS G12C inhibitor used in preclinical combination studies

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal timing and sequencing of KRAS inhibitor plus checkpoint blockade to maximize durable responses?
Can the immune response generated by allele‑specific KRAS inhibition be enhanced without increasing toxicity?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Intrinsic mechanisms of resistance to RAS inhibitors
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we have intrinsic resistance due to KEAP1, SMARCA4, and CDKN2N alterations"
Speaker said: "acquired alterations in cis at residues 95, 96, 68 impair drug binding"
Speaker said: "adaptive resistance involves transcriptional rewiring and wild‑type RAS activation"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Co‑occurring KEAP1, SMARCA4, CDKN2N alterations that blunt response to monotherapy
In‑cis mutations at KRAS residues 95, 96, 68 that prevent covalent binding
Transcriptional rewiring leading to increased GTP‑bound KRAS and activation of wild‑type RAS signaling

trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling KRAS G12C patients without stratifying for co‑occurring KEAP1/SMARCA4/CDKN2N alterations
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering to detect benefit in biomarker‑negative subcohort

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker noted that the covalent cysteine handle limits activity to G12C and does not address other KRAS alleles
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Focusing on G12C monotherapy without upfront combination leads to rapid resistance

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed preclinical data that SOS1 or MEK combinations dramatically improve tumor control (observation) but concluded that “we still need many trials to test all these combos” without presenting a clear prioritization framework (contradiction)

key_data_gaps: Lack of clinical outcome data for proposed combos
No patient‑level biomarker validation for KEAP1/SMARCA4/CDKN2N

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop CrisPRO‑based predictive models to identify patients with KEAP1/SMARCA4/CDKN2N co‑mutations who may need upfront combination therapy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted these co‑alterations as major determinants of inferior outcome to KRAS G12C monotherapy
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate multi‑omics to generate a resistance risk score for trial enrichment

crispro_opportunity.description: Prioritize SOS1 or SHP2 inhibitors as rational partners for KRAS G12C inhibitors based on preclinical synergy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker presented in‑vivo data showing SOS1 or MEK combos outperform monotherapy
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate pathway flux to predict optimal upstream/downstream partner selection

cited_competitors.name: sotorasib
cited_competitors.context: Approved KRAS G12C inhibitor used as reference

cited_competitors.name: adagrasib
cited_competitors.context: Approved KRAS G12C inhibitor used as reference

unresolved_questions: Which upstream (SOS1, SHP2) or downstream (MEK, CDK4/6) partner yields the best therapeutic index in KRAS G12C patients with KEAP1 loss?
How can adaptive transcriptional rewiring be monitored in real time to guide therapy switches?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Hippo‑YAP/TAZ pathway as a driver of adaptive resistance and immune evasion
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "adaptive activation of Hippo‑YAP/TAZ pathway can be a mechanism of resistance"
Speaker said: "FAK inhibition combined with avotamgenib showed synergistic tumor reduction"
Speaker said: "RND3‑RhoE loop links ERK inhibition to YAP activation"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Rapid up‑regulation of FAK and YAP target genes after ERK inhibition via RND3 repression
FAK inhibition does not fully suppress YAP‑driven transcription; residual YAP activity persists

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Combination of MEK inhibitor (avotamgenib) + FAK inhibitor
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Enrolling all‑comer melanoma patients without selecting for Hippo‑YAP activation status
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Baseline YAP/TAZ activity or phospho‑FAK levels
trial_dilution_risk.static_vs_dynamic_failure: No serial biopsies to track emergence of YAP activation

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker noted early‑stage clinical data with modest PFS benefit and reversible kidney toxicity, suggesting limited therapeutic window
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Relying on TED inhibitors as single agents yields cytostatic rather than cytotoxic effects

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that avotamgenib strongly suppresses ERK re‑activation (observation) but also admitted that “MEK inhibitors still cause toxicity and limited durability” (contradiction)

Speaker demonstrated robust preclinical synergy of FAK + TED inhibition (observation) yet concluded that “we still need biomarkers to know when to use these combos” without presenting candidate biomarkers (contradiction)

key_data_gaps: No randomized clinical data for MEK+FAK+TED triple combination
Lack of patient‑level YAP activation biomarkers
Limited safety data for chronic TED inhibition

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to predict YAP/TAZ target gene up‑regulation after MEK inhibition and pre‑emptively add FAK/TED inhibitors
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker described rapid YAP target induction and FAK activation following avotamgenib treatment
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model transcriptional rewiring to flag patients likely to develop YAP‑driven resistance

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a CrisPRO‑derived YAP activity score to select patients for MEK+FAK+TED combination trials
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted absence of baseline YAP/FAK biomarkers in current trial designs
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate RNA‑seq and phospho‑proteomics to generate a predictive YAP activation signature

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify synergistic partners for TED inhibitors beyond FAK, such as immune checkpoint blockade, using CrisPRO network analysis
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker mentioned ongoing work combining TED inhibitors with anti‑PD‑1 in preclinical models
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate multi‑node perturbations to prioritize safe and effective combos

cited_competitors.name: avotamgenib
cited_competitors.context: MEK inhibitor described as a ‘glue’ that stabilizes RAF‑MEK complex

cited_competitors.name: darovassartib
cited_competitors.context: FAK inhibitor used in combination studies

cited_competitors.name: TED inhibitors (e.g., VX‑970)
cited_competitors.context: Targeting YAP/TAZ‑TEAD interaction, early clinical data presented

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal sequencing of MEK, FAK, and TED inhibition to minimize toxicity?
Can YAP/TAZ activation be reliably measured in circulating tumor DNA or liquid biopsies for real‑time monitoring?
